rationale: I split Humanities into 14 practitioner-facing subspecialties to balance breadth (major recognizable disciplines) with granularity for common research identities. I separated closely related but distinct professional identities that frequently map to different journals and conferences — for example History vs History of Science, and Philosophy vs Philosophy of Science — while keeping literary fields and cultural/media fields distinct because they attract different methods and audiences. I deliberately did not create many hyper‑narrow historical periods or regional area studies cards (those are searchable tags inside the app) and avoided digital humanities as it routes to Computer Science & AI per the constraints. A few borderline splits (Comparative Literature vs Literary Studies) were kept because practitioners commonly self-identify with those specific titles.

subspecialties.name: History
subspecialties.tagline: Political, social, economic, and cultural history
covers: national and transnational history
social history, political history, economic history

subspecialties.name: History of Science
subspecialties.tagline: Historical study of science, technology, and medicine
covers: science & medicine histories, technology studies
biographies of scientists, laboratory histories

subspecialties.name: Philosophy
subspecialties.tagline: Ethics, metaphysics, epistemology, and analytic/continental work
covers: moral and political philosophy, metaphysics, epistemology
continental, analytic, and applied philosophy

subspecialties.name: Philosophy of Science
subspecialties.tagline: Philosophical analysis of science, models, and explanation
covers: theory choice, scientific explanation, models, measurement
philosophy of particular sciences and methods

subspecialties.name: Archaeology
subspecialties.tagline: Material culture, excavation reports, and field archaeology
covers: excavation results, artefact analysis, landscape archaeology
bioarchaeology, dating methods, material studies

subspecialties.name: Classics
subspecialties.tagline: Ancient Greek and Roman language, literature, and history
covers: Latin and Greek philology, ancient history, classical literature
epigraphy, textual criticism of classical texts

subspecialties.name: Art History
subspecialties.tagline: Visual art, architecture, and material culture studies
covers: painting, sculpture, architectural history
visual culture, iconography, connoisseurship

subspecialties.name: Literary Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Literature analysis, theory, and text-based scholarship
covers: close reading, literary theory, period literature studies
genre studies, author studies, textual criticism

subspecialties.name: Comparative Literature
covers: translation studies, world literature, interliterary comparison
comparative theory across languages and cultures

subspecialties.name: Linguistics
subspecialties.tagline: Language structure, historical linguistics, and philology
covers: syntax, phonology, semantics, sociolinguistics
historical linguistics and philological methods

subspecialties.name: Religious Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Comparative religion, theology, and ritual studies
covers: textual and ethnographic study of religions
theology, ritual, sacred texts, religion and society

subspecialties.name: Cultural Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Critical studies of culture, identity, and ideology
covers: race, gender, postcolonial studies, ideology critique
popular culture analysis and cultural theory

subspecialties.name: Film & Media Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Cinema, television, and media analysis and theory
covers: film theory, media history, audience studies
television studies, digital media criticism (non‑computational)

subspecialties.name: Museum & Heritage Studies
subspecialties.tagline: Museology, conservation, and public history practice
covers: museum curation, heritage policy, conservation science (non‑technical)
public history, exhibition studies

uncovered_concerns: Area/regional studies (e.g., East Asian Studies) were considered but kept out as separate level-2; these are common research tags rather than orthogonal professional identities.

Digital Humanities intentionally excluded due to hard constraint routing those papers to Computer Science & AI; many borderline DH works should show under the closest methodological field (e.g., linguistics, media-studies) with a tag.

Performance studies, rhetoric/composition, and translation studies are real practitioner identities but split thinly across institutions; they are better surfaced as tags or combined under Cultural Studies / Comparative Literature depending on focus.
Epigraphy/paleography and manuscript studies are important but represent niche professional labels; they will be discoverable via subject tags rather than primary picker cards.
